title: One sentence runs the whole system
paras: One sentence runs the whole system. Every determination in England runs off section 38, subsection 6 of the Planning and Compulsory Purchase Act 2004. The application must be determined in accordance with the development plan unless material considerations indicate otherwise. That clause fixes the method. You don't begin with the Framework, the objections, or the outcome you'd like. You begin by reading the plan as a whole, and forming a view on whether the proposal accords with it. Only then do you ask whether material considerations point the other way. Reverse those two steps, and the decision is vulnerable — however sensible the result.

Section 38(6) is reinforced by section 70, subsection 2 of the Town and Country Planning Act 1990. That provision requires the authority, in dealing with an application, to have regard to the development plan, to any local finance considerations, and to any other material considerations. Together, they impose a two-stage method. Stage one is the plan position. Read the development plan as a whole, identify the policies genuinely relevant to the proposal, and reach a view — in accordance with the plan, or in conflict, taken overall. A scheme can breach one policy and satisfy others. The statutory question is the balance across the plan, not a single clause.

Stage two is the tug of war. Only now do the Framework and the other material considerations enter, and they come in to answer one question: do they indicate a decision otherwise than in accordance with the plan? And what is the development plan? It comprises the adopted local plan or plans. Any made neighbourhood plan that has passed referendum — which then forms part of the statutory plan, and carries full weight, rather than the weight of a mere material consideration. Minerals and waste plans. And, where relevant, the spatial development strategy — which in London is the London Plan. Where two plan documents conflict, section 38, subsection 5 resolves it: the later-adopted policy prevails.

The plan-led system is not a presumption that the plan is right. It's a discipline that the plan is the starting point. It can be outweighed — but only after it has been correctly identified. An officer who can't say, in one sentence, whether the proposal accords with the plan taken as a whole hasn't yet started the decision.

title: Materiality is law; weight is judgement
paras: Materiality is law; weight is judgement. Two questions are constantly confused here, and separating them is what marks out a planner who decides from one who only quotes policy. Whether a consideration is material is a question of law: it's material if it relates to the use and development of land and serves a planning purpose — that's Stringer. How much weight it carries is a question of planning judgement. And provided the consideration is material and the decision rational, weight is for the decision-maker, not the courts — that's Tesco against the Secretary of State. Which is why two competent planners can weigh the same facts differently, and both be lawful.

The materiality test comes from Stringer against the Minister of Housing and Local Government, reported at 1970, 1 W-L-R 1281. A consideration is capable of being material if it serves a planning purpose, and a planning purpose relates to the character of the use of land. Whether something is capable of being material is, ultimately, for the court.

Weight is different in kind. In Tesco Stores Limited against the Secretary of State for the Environment — reported at 1995, 1 W-L-R 759 — the House of Lords held that once a consideration is material, the weight given to it is entirely for the decision-maker, subject only to Wednesbury rationality. The court polices the boundary of materiality; it does not re-weigh. A third distinction completes the set: interpretation of policy is law, application is judgement. The meaning of a development-plan policy is an objective question of law, read in context — that's Tesco Stores Limited against Dundee City Council, reported at 2012, U-K-S-C 13. But how the correctly interpreted policy applies to the facts, and the weight the resulting compliance or conflict attracts — that's judgement. On a statutory challenge, this is decisive. Interpretation can be fought as law; weight can be attacked only on rationality.

The reading worth carrying into practice is purposive. Policies are instruments for outcomes, not ends in themselves. Ask what a policy is for — what harm it guards against, what objective it serves. A proposal may breach the letter of a policy while leaving its purpose unharmed, and that weakens the weight of the conflict. Or it may comply with the letter in a way that defeats the purpose, which weakens the value of the compliance. That's where judgement earns its keep — and where a technical breach can be argued down to a neutral, or even a positive, finding.

title: When the plan, the Framework and a minister disagree
paras: So what happens when the plan, the Framework and a minister disagree? When they appear to say different things, it can feel like chaos. It isn't. It's resolved by legal status, then rational weight, then reasons. Nothing lower in the ladder overrides something higher as a matter of law; the argument is almost always about weight, not authority. Get the status of each source right, and most apparent conflicts dissolve into a weighting exercise you can reason through and defend.

Here's the ladder, by status. Statute sits above everything — the Acts, and in particular the statutory duties that are not mere material considerations but mandatory obligations: sections 66 and 72 of the Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990, and the Conservation of Habitats and Species Regulations 2017, which impose hard tests that are not simply weighed away. Beneath statute is the development plan — the section 38(6) starting point — which policy and guidance do not override as a matter of law.

The National Planning Policy Framework is government policy, and a material consideration of great weight. But it is not statute, and it is not part of the development plan. It does not change the statutory primacy of the plan. That was settled in Hopkins Homes Limited against the Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government, and Richborough Estates against Cheshire East Borough Council, reported at 2017, U-K-S-C 37, where the Supreme Court held that the presumption operates through the weight given to plan policies — not by displacing the statute. So the complaint that the Framework says something different is usually, really, the question: how out of date is the relevant plan policy? Below the Framework sits Planning Practice Guidance, which explains how to apply it, carries less weight, and cannot contradict it.

Ministerial statements belong on a gradient. A formal Written Ministerial Statement is a material consideration, and it can carry real weight — onshore wind, Article 4 directions and housing delivery have all been steered by such statements ahead of the Framework. But it does not amend the development plan, and it cannot override statute. Below it, a recovered-appeal or call-in decision indicates the direction of ministerial thinking, but weighs less. A speech or a press release barely registers. Emerging and neighbourhood plans take weight by the stage reached, the extent of unresolved objections, and their consistency with the Framework.

Two further sources complete the picture. Previous appeal decisions matter, because consistency is itself a consideration. A decision-maker who departs from a relevant earlier appeal decision on similar facts must explain why — that's North Wiltshire District Council against the Secretary of State for the Environment, reported at 1992, 65 P-and-C-R 137 — though each case turns on its facts, and an appeal decision is not a binding precedent. And supplementary planning documents, design codes and local guidance are material, with weight varying by status and currency. The takeaway is a single sequence: identify legal status, apply rational weight, then give clear reasons.

title: The tilted balance, and how to spot it
paras: The tilted balance, and how to spot it. This is the single most consequential switch in English decision-making, and knowing when it's engaged is a core skill. In decision-taking, where there is no relevant development-plan policy, or the policies most important for determining the application are out of date, the exercise reframes: permission should be granted unless the adverse impacts would significantly and demonstrably outweigh the benefits, assessed against the Framework as a whole. That is a materially more applicant-friendly test than “does this accord with the plan” — and recognising the trigger is often worth more than any single substantive argument.

The presumption in favour of sustainable development has two limbs: plan-making, and decision-taking. The decision-taking limb engages the tilted balance in two situations — where there are no relevant plan policies, or where the policies most important for the decision are out of date. In the housing context, the most important policies are treated as out of date where the authority cannot demonstrate the required supply of deliverable housing sites. But the supply period, the buffers, and the way this is expressed change between editions of the Framework — so check the current wording, rather than recalling it.

Two carve-outs must be checked before you conclude that the balance is tilted in the applicant's favour. First, the tilt doesn't apply where specific Framework policies that protect particular assets provide a clear reason for refusing — the protections the Framework lists in its own footnote, which typically include habitats sites, sites of special scientific interest, designated heritage assets, land at risk of flooding, and protected landscapes. Second, even where it is engaged, the tilt is a balance and not a grant. The adverse impacts can still prevail, if they significantly and demonstrably outweigh the benefits.

In plain terms, the tilt removes plan-conflict as the organising question, and substitutes a benefits-against-harms test weighted towards approval. The sequence runs like this. Establish the plan position anyway, because it still frames weight. Test whether the presumption is engaged, including the supply position. Check the protective policies that can supply a clear reason for refusal. And if the tilt applies and no such policy bites, apply the significantly-and-demonstrably test against the Framework as a whole. This connects back to Hopkins Homes: the tilt is the mechanism by which out-of-date plan policies lose weight. It is not a freestanding override of the statute. It is section 38(6) operating through weight — exactly as the Supreme Court described.

title: Duties, not considerations
paras: Duties, not considerations. Some things are weighed; some things constrain. Statutory duties — heritage and habitats chief among them — do not go onto the scales as ordinary material considerations. They operate as thresholds, or as strong presumptions the proposal must clear before the ordinary balance is reached. Miss this, and you can build a tidy benefits-against-harms table that is unlawful — because it has weighed away a duty that was never yours to weigh.

Heritage first. Sections 66, subsection 1, and 72, subsection 1, of the Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990 require the decision-maker to have special regard to preserving a listed building and its setting, and to pay special attention to preserving or enhancing the character and appearance of conservation areas. The courts have held that these duties attract considerable importance and weight — that's Barnwell Manor Wind Energy Limited against East Northamptonshire District Council, reported at 2014, E-W-C-A Civ 137 — and that harm to a designated asset is not simply one factor in a level balance. It carries a strong statutory presumption against grant, which must be clearly outweighed. The detail belongs in the heritage guide; the point here is structural. It is a duty, not a consideration.

Then habitats. The Conservation of Habitats and Species Regulations 2017 impose hard tests around European sites. Where a plan or project is likely to have a significant effect, the authority must carry out an appropriate assessment — and may only agree to the project once it has ascertained that it will not adversely affect the integrity of the site, save through the strict imperative-reasons derogation. This is a threshold, not a weighting. A proposal that fails it cannot be permitted, however great its benefits.

Nutrient neutrality, and recreational impacts on European sites, remain fast-moving — the subject of Natural England advice, litigation, and repeated legislative attention — so check the position on the file, rather than stating it from memory. The general lesson holds, though. Where a statutory duty or a hard test applies, run it first, as a gateway, and only reach the ordinary planning balance for the matters genuinely to be weighed. A benefits-against-harms table that has silently absorbed a section 66 duty, or an appropriate-assessment failure, is not a balance. It's an error of law dressed as one.

title: The reasons are the decision
paras: The reasons are the decision. The discipline of writing the reasons is the discipline of making the decision. If you can't articulate why the benefits outweigh the harms, you haven't finished weighing them — you've reached a conclusion and dressed it. Reasons must be intelligible and adequate, and they must grapple with the principal important controversial issues. A decision with a defensible outcome but hollow reasons is a decision waiting to be quashed.

The standard is set by South Bucks District Council against Porter, number 2, reported at 2004, U-K-H-L 33. Reasons must be intelligible and adequate. They must enable the reader to understand why the matter was decided as it was, and what was concluded on the principal important controversial issues. And they must not give rise to a substantial doubt that the decision-maker erred. Reasons can be briefly stated — the degree of particularity depends on the issues.

For local planning authorities, the reasons duty is not confined to refusals. The routine statutory requirement to give full reasons for grants was pared back, yes. But the courts have held that a common-law duty to give reasons can arise — notably where members grant against officer advice, on a matter of significant public interest, or where the reasoning is otherwise not apparent. That's R, on the application of C-P-R-E Kent, against Dover District Council, reported at 2017, U-K-S-C 79. The safe assumption in committee work is this: a departure from the officer recommendation must be minuted with intelligible planning reasons, at the time.

The craft point is to write the balance as a sequence of reasoned weightings, not as a conclusion. State each main consideration, the weight given, and why. Address the contrary points. Then reach the overall conclusion. An officer report or committee minute built this way is both a better decision and a far harder target on challenge — because it shows the principal controversial issues were confronted, rather than glossed.

title: Things that feel material and aren’t
paras: Things that feel material — and aren't. Arguing the wrong points loses credibility and wastes committee time. A short list of objections is reliably non-material — a private view, property values, boundary and covenant disputes, competition, the applicant's identity. But several are non-material only until a genuine planning consequence is shown. The skill isn't memorising the list. It's spotting the moment a red herring acquires a land-use consequence, and becomes material after all.

The reliably non-material ones first. Loss of a private view — there is no right to a view. The effect on property values. Boundary, covenant and other private legal disputes. Loss of trade to a competitor — competition is not a planning consideration, though the planning consequences of vacancy or lost vitality may be. The identity, character or motives of the applicant. And matters controlled by other regimes — building regulations, licensing, environmental permitting — except where there is a genuine land-use overlap.

Then the ones that are non-material until a planning consequence appears. Construction-period disturbance — temporary, and usually controllable by condition, but it can carry weight where prolonged or acute. Fear and perceived risk — exceptionally, these can be material where perception has a real effect on the use and enjoyment of land, even if the underlying fear is objectively unfounded. And need for the development — not generally required, but it can add positive weight, and it's central to some policy tests, such as rural-worker dwellings.

Three considerations are genuinely material but routinely mishandled. First, the fallback. That's a realistic alternative the applicant could carry out without the permission sought — extant permitted-development rights, or an extant planning permission — and its weight depends on how real the prospect is. A real prospect of a comparable but less desirable outcome can be decisive, particularly for householder and change-of-use cases. Second, precedent. It's capable of being material, but it must be evidenced, not asserted. The decision-maker must show a real difficulty in resisting like cases, and a bare floodgates argument is routinely rejected. Third, prematurity. That's narrowly confined by guidance — rarely justified except where the proposal is so substantial, and the emerging plan so advanced, that granting would predetermine central decisions. It seldom succeeds, and should be argued sparingly. And one further discipline for committee work: the volume of public objection is not itself material. The planning merits of the objections are — their sheer number is not. A report that counts letters instead of weighing points invites challenge.

title: The considerations scale with the proposal
paras: The considerations scale with the proposal. The doctrine in the topics above is one framework, but it doesn't apply identically to every application. As a proposal grows, the number of material considerations grows. The decisive question climbs — from amenity, to principle, to the overall planning balance and the housing-supply position. And statutory duties and the tilted balance can displace the ordinary plan-led starting point. The topics that follow work the framework through eight common application types, from a householder extension to a major greenfield scheme.

Two things are worth holding in mind across all of them. First, the typical weights suggested are practice and judgement, not law — calibrate them to the development plan and the evidence in front of you. Second, the skill is not listing every consideration that's present; it's identifying which are decisive. An extension is decided on amenity and character. A countryside dwelling, on the principle. A major scheme, on the overall planning balance and whether the tilt is engaged. Read down to the type nearest your case — but answer the decisive question first.

title: Applying it: the householder extension
paras: Applying it, then — starting with the householder extension. At an existing dwelling, the principle of development is not in issue. A householder extension is won or lost on two things: the character of the dwelling and the street scene, and the amenity of the neighbours. Everything else is usually secondary.

On character, the questions are scale, proportion, materials, and whether the extension is subservient to the host dwelling — and this is usually the decisive strand. On amenity, weigh overlooking and loss of privacy; loss of light and overshadowing — that's the 45-degree and 25-degree rules of thumb, and the B-R-E daylight and sunlight guidance where it's genuinely in issue; and any overbearing or enclosing effect. Highways and parking rarely decide a householder scheme, and trees carry weight only where amenity trees or a Tree Preservation Order are affected. There's no statutory duty unless the dwelling is listed or in a conservation area — in which case the sections 66 and 72 duties engage.

The genuinely material point practitioners most often forget is the permitted-development fallback. Where the applicant has a real prospect of building something comparable without consent, that fallback is a material consideration, and it's frequently decisive. Against it, dismiss the reliable red herrings: loss of a private view, effect on property value, boundary or covenant disputes, and a neighbour's own extension invoked as precedent. The decisive question is whether the extension is subservient to the host dwelling, and protects the neighbours' living conditions. Conditions typically cover the time limit, matching materials, and obscure or non-opening windows to protect privacy. Obligations — usually none.

title: Applying it: a new house in a settlement
paras: Next, applying it to a new house in a settlement. A single new dwelling on an infill or garden plot adds a principle-and-location layer on top of the amenity questions of an extension. The first question is whether the principle of a new dwelling is acceptable in this location — inside the settlement boundary, in a sustainable spot, and whether the plan supports or resists infill.

Once the principle is arguable, the design must sit in its context: grain, density, plot pattern, and whether the scheme reads as sympathetic infill — or as garden-grabbing and town-cramming. Neighbour amenity is assessed in both directions: to the neighbours, and for the future occupiers, whose living conditions — private amenity space, light, outlook — are themselves material. Then add access and highway safety: visibility, parking, servicing. Foul and surface-water drainage, and the flood-risk sequential test where the site is in a flood zone. Trees, and any Tree Preservation Order. And ecology, including mandatory biodiversity net gain. Heritage duties engage if the site is designated, or affects a designated asset's setting. Habitats — only if near a European site.

The decisive question is whether the principle is acceptable in this location, and whether the design sits comfortably in its context. Precedent is routinely raised here, and usually overstated. Loss of view, property value, and the applicant's personal need are not material. Conditions typically cover approved plans, materials, landscaping, drainage and SuDS, ecology and biodiversity net gain, and obscure glazing. Obligations may secure off-site net gain, and the Community Infrastructure Levy applies where the authority charges — though affordable housing is usually below threshold on a small site.

title: Applying it: a new house in the countryside
paras: Now, applying it to a new house in the countryside. A new house in open countryside is decided on the principle — and almost nothing else matters until the principle is cleared. New isolated homes in the countryside are resisted, save for a narrow set of exceptions, and a scheme that doesn't fall within one of them will not be saved by good design or amenity.

The Framework's isolated-homes policy allows limited exceptions. An essential need for a rural worker to live at the site. The re-use or redevelopment of a redundant or disused building. An optimal-viability heritage case. A dwelling of truly outstanding design. And the sub-division of an existing dwelling. Whether a site is “isolated” is itself a question of fact and judgement. It means physically isolated, not functionally isolated — that's Braintree District Council against the Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government, reported at 2018, E-W-C-A Civ 610 — so a dwelling near a hamlet may not be isolated at all, which changes the policy that applies. Only if the principle is arguable do the usual strands follow: landscape and character, heritage setting, highways, amenity, drainage, and ecology.

Statutory duties often bite here. Heritage setting, under sections 66 and 72. The great weight owed to a protected landscape — a National Landscape, or Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. And habitats, where a European site is near. The applicant's wish to live rurally, family or personal circumstances, and abstract “need” carry little or no weight. The decisive question is whether the proposal falls within one of the recognised exceptions. Where it does, the usual conditions are an occupancy condition for a rural-worker case, removal of permitted-development rights, landscaping and materials — occasionally with a section 106 tie to a holding.

title: Applying it: the rural worker’s dwelling
paras: Applying it to the rural worker's dwelling. This turns on whether there is an essential need for a worker to live permanently at or near the workplace — and whether the enterprise that generates that need is financially sound. Functional need is the gateway. Does the enterprise genuinely require a worker readily available on site, for animal welfare or security?

Beyond functional need, the enterprise must be established and capable of sustaining the dwelling — a new enterprise is usually tested by a temporary permission first. A critical question is whether the need could be met by accommodation already on or near the holding. If it can, the case usually fails. If those gates are passed, siting must be tied to the holding, the dwelling proportionate to the need, and the usual strands of design, amenity, highways and drainage assessed. Heritage, habitats or landscape duties apply where engaged.

The applicant's preference to live on site, retirement or succession planning, and equity release are not the point, and carry little weight. The decisive question is whether there is a proven essential functional need that existing accommodation cannot meet — and whether the enterprise is financially sound. Conditions typically include an agricultural occupancy condition, a tie to the holding, removal of permitted-development rights, and, frequently, a temporary consent first. And cross-refer the conditions and section 73 guides on removing an agricultural occupancy condition later.

title: Applying it: a change of use
paras: Applying it to a change of use. The first move here is to check whether permission is even needed — the flexibility within the Use Classes Order, and the prior-approval routes of Class M-A and Class Q, may mean it isn't. If permission is required, the twin questions are whether the loss of the existing use is acceptable, and whether the new use is acceptable in this location.

The loss of the existing use — employment land, a community facility, or retail vitality — carries weight that depends on demand evidence and marketing. An authority resisting the loss of employment land without that evidence is on weak ground. The acceptability of the new use turns on its amenity impacts — noise, hours, odour, traffic — and its compatibility with neighbours, with highways, flood risk and contamination assessed as relevant. For main town-centre uses in edge or out-of-centre locations, the sequential test and any impact test apply. Heritage duties engage if the building is listed or in a conservation area. Habitats — rarely.

Competition, or loss of trade to a rival, is not material. Nor is the identity of the operator, or matters for licensing or environmental permitting, absent a genuine land-use overlap. The decisive question is whether the loss of the existing use is justified, and whether the new use is compatible with its surroundings. Conditions commonly control hours of use, noise and odour, and parking and servicing — and they may restrict the Use Classes flexibility where justified.

title: Applying it: a replacement dwelling
paras: Applying it to a replacement dwelling. A replacement dwelling in the countryside turns first on a threshold question — is there an existing lawful dwelling capable of replacement? A dwelling whose residential use has been abandoned, or which was never lawful, cannot simply be replaced. Abandonment and lawfulness are the first things to establish.

If there is a lawful dwelling, the questions become scale and siting. Many plans resist a disproportionate increase in size over the original dwelling, and landscape impact rises with bulk. The replacement should sit on or near the existing footprint — because a materially different position is a new dwelling in disguise. Landscape and character, amenity, highways and drainage follow. And ecology matters. Protected species — particularly bats — are common in rural buildings, and they operate as a hard constraint under the Wildlife and Countryside Act 1981 and the Habitats Regulations 2017, requiring survey and, where needed, a licence. Heritage duties apply if the building is designated.

A simple wish for a larger house, and the usual view and value points, are not material. The decisive question is whether there is a lawful dwelling to replace, and whether the replacement is proportionate — and no more harmful to the countryside than what it replaces. Conditions typically require demolition of the existing dwelling before, or within a set period of, occupation; removal of permitted-development rights; materials; and ecological mitigation. Cross-refer the lawful development certificate guide on abandonment.

title: Applying it: a minor commercial scheme
paras: Applying it to a minor commercial scheme. A minor commercial or employment proposal is decided on whether the use is acceptable in this location, and whether it can protect residential amenity and highway safety. Amenity is usually the reason such schemes are refused in or near residential areas.

On amenity, weigh noise, hours, odour, light and dust — especially where sensitive receptors adjoin. On highways, assess access, parking, servicing and manoeuvring. Economic benefit — jobs and investment — carries positive weight in proportion to scale. Loss of any other use, design, flood risk and contamination are assessed as relevant. Heritage duties engage if the building is designated; habitats, rarely. And for main town-centre uses, the sequential test applies.

Competition, the operator's identity, and matters for licensing or environmental permitting — absent a land-use overlap — are not material. The decisive question is whether the use can coexist with its neighbours, and with the highway network, without unacceptable harm. Conditions commonly control hours, noise limits, delivery and servicing times, parking, and the use itself — with a travel plan for larger schemes.

title: Applying it: 150 homes on a greenfield edge
paras: And finally, applying it to 150 homes on a greenfield edge. A major greenfield housing scheme brings the full apparatus into play, and the decision becomes an explicit planning balance. The principle comes first — is housing here acceptable, allocated or not, within or beyond the settlement boundary, and consistent with the spatial strategy? And then the pivotal question: is the tilted balance engaged?

Settle the plan position, then test the housing land supply position, and whether the presumption is engaged — see the tilted-balance topic above. If it is, the exercise reframes from “does this accord with the plan” to whether the harms significantly and demonstrably outweigh the benefits, against the Framework as a whole — after checking whether a protective policy supplies a clear reason for refusal. The benefits to weigh explicitly are these. Market and affordable housing — weight in proportion to need and any shortfall, often substantial where supply is short. Economic activity. Infrastructure and contributions. Biodiversity net gain. And any public-realm or highway improvements. Now the harms. Landscape and visual impact — and whether a protected landscape is engaged, which attracts great weight. Loss of best and most versatile agricultural land. Heritage, including the setting of designated assets. Highways — recalling the Framework's “severe” residual-cumulative-impact threshold, and the separate safety test. Ecology. Flood risk and drainage. And coalescence and character.

The statutory duties — habitats, with appropriate assessment where a likely significant effect arises, and heritage, with its considerable importance and weight — sit outside the balance as constraints, not items on the scales. Section 106 obligations must each meet the Regulation 122 tests: necessary; directly related; and fairly and reasonably related in scale and kind. And the Community Infrastructure Levy applies where the authority charges. There's a process overlay too: environmental impact assessment screening for Schedule 2 development against the thresholds; likely committee determination, and call-in risk; and the correct treatment of the volume of objection — the merits of objections are material, the number is not. The decisive question is whether the tilted balance is engaged and, if so, whether the harms significantly and demonstrably outweigh the substantial benefit of housing delivery.
